text: During the laparoscopic appendectomy, the surgeon identifies the cecum and its tinea coli, noting its mobility. The terminal ileum is lifted to reveal the appendix, which is positioned in the pelvis. The base of the appendix is located where the three tinea coli converge.

The surgeon uses blunt dissection to release the inflamed appendix tip from pelvic adhesions. The appendix is stuck in the pelvis, showing signs of severe inflammation and contained perforation, with visible fibrinous flecks and omentum surrounding the area.

The surgeon uses a grasper in the left hand to hold the appendix while dissecting the thick and edematous mesoappendix with a hook. Traction and counter-traction are applied to carefully dissect the mesoappendix bit by bit, adjusting the traction as needed.

The surgeon uses the back of the hook to dissect the mesoappendix, applying traction and counter-traction with the left hand to carefully separate the thick, edematous tissue. Smoke is released to maintain visibility, and the dissection continues slowly to prevent bleeding, allowing the mesoappendix to fall away and expose the base of the appendix.
The surgeon carefully manipulates the appendix, guiding it down to the base where the three tinea coli converge. The cecum is visible, and caution is exercised to avoid injury to this structure.
The surgeon uses a handmade 2-0 Vicryl loop to secure the appendix, creating an extracorporeal knot and pushing the loop into place with a Maryland dissector.
The surgeon uses the Maryland dissector to push the knot down onto the base of the appendix at the confluence of the tinea coli, securing it in place.

The surgeon carefully secures the knot at the base of the appendix, ensuring it is neither too loose nor too tight to prevent avulsion. The knot creates a visible dumbbell shape, with blanching observed around the appendix. Using either a hook or scissors, the surgeon prepares to cut the appendix, ensuring not to leave a large stump to avoid stump appendicitis. The procedure is viewed under six times magnification.

The surgeon removes the appendix through the left iliac fossa using a 10 mm port. The left hand grasper is released, and the appendix is taken out with the right hand. The right iliac fossa is irrigated, and the pelvis appears clean. The stump is visibly well secured.
The surgeon removes the 10 mm trocar from the left iliac fossa under direct vision, ensuring there is no port site bleeding. Subsequently, the 5 mm suprapubic trocar is also removed.
